title: practical english usage - Part 340
content:
It's not on this shelf; it's on the next shelf up.

We get off at the next station. (= the station that we  will come  to next.) It means ‘ after this/that one'.

The next can be used for place if we  are talking about movement or direction.

If you want to find Luke, just look in the nearest pub.

Excuse me. Where's the nearest bank? (     . . . the next bank.) The nearest is generally preferred for place - it means ‘ most near in space'.

4 place:   next     and   nearest 538 vocabulary •          now (that) Now the exams are over I can enjoy myself.

Now (that) Andrew is married, he has become much  more responsible.

dropped, especially in British English (►  265).

Now (that) can be used as a conjunction. In an informal style, that is often 538 now     (that) Forty-love; Andrews to serve.

And the score at half-time is: Scotland three, England nil.

English. Zero is followed by a plural noun.

In measurements of temperature, 0 is called zero in both British and American or My account number is four one three oh six.  . . . four one three zero six.

oh (like the letter O).

American English. When  we  say numbers one figure at a time, 0 is often called The  figure 0 is usually called nought or zero in British English and zero in 537 nought,        zero,     nil,   etc There wasn't an answer. / There was no answer.

meanings. The  structure with no is usually more emphatic.

verb        +                + noun Sometimes sentences constructed with            not and no    have similar NO  SMOKING I telephoned, but there was no answer. (= There wasn't an answer.) I've got no Thursdays free this term. (= ɪ  haven’ t got any Thursdays ...) No teachers went on strike. (= There weren't any teachers on strike.) We   can use no with a noun or -ing form to mean 'not any’ , or 'not a/an'.

I have not received his answer.

/ can see you tomorrow, but not on Thursday.

The students went on strike, but not the teachers, (     . . .-but no the4eachers?) not Not surprisingly, we missed the train, (     No surprisingly .. .) To  make a word, expression or clause negative, we  use not.

Note that often has two common pronunciations, with and without t. /'ofən/ or I fell several times yesterday when I was skiing,  I kept falling yesterday...

I often fell in love when I was younger.

expression (e.g. a lot of times, several times, keep.. .ing). Compare: occasions’ . To  say ‘ a lot of times on one occasion', we  normally use another Often is mostly used for habitual behaviour, to mean ‘ a lot of times on different 541  often     ______________ For the use of of course to structure arguments, ►    284.2. 301.3.

speaker had said something too obvious to be worth mentioning.) not ‘ It's cold.' ‘ It certainly is.' (     Ofcourse-it^ - this would suggest that the first But of course is not always a very polite reply to a statement of fact.

‘ Couldyou help me? ‘ Of  course.' Of  course can be used as a polite reply to a request.

We'll leave at eight o'clock. Granny won't be coming, of course.

It looks as if the sun goes around the earth, but of course that's not true.

We use of course (not) to mean ‘ as everybody knows' or ‘ as is obvious'.

I don't like modern fashions, (     I don't-like-the-nowadays fashions-.) Nowadays cannot be used as an adjective.

Nowadays we think nothing of space travel.

People seem to be very depressed nowadays.
